==Journal==
{{Journal entry|
The bronze guard defended the alliance between the followers of Guthix and the followers of Saradomin. The metal symbolized the unity of two different materials and created something stronger. While most of the guards preferred the noble sword, a few preferred the physicality of the bronze mace. 

Each swing delivered powerful momentum straight into their enemy. The force broke bone, obliterated muscle, and often sent their victims reeling, allowing a second strike before the foe could recover. 

There was a mountain of a man known as The Cairn, for his skill at delivering people to one, who favoured the mace over more noble weapons. He crushed anyone who defied the alliance, providing the force of justice with each swing of the mace. 

When he was asked to turn his fury on the priests of Guthix, it was the one time he stayed his hand.
}}

{{Journal entry|
Copper and tin conspire well together, forging an alliance to destroy their former friends. The bronze pickaxe can shatter the mightiest stones and can mine through more durable ore than bone and stone could ever dream to battle.

The dwarves that Guthix brought with him to Ashenfall, were fond of the bronze pickaxe. While iron and steel are sturdier, the bronze pickaxe was more readily available, and they could risk a little decoration. Many would customise their pickaxes with symbols of their family crests, or a nickname that embodied them well. Often these would make the pickaxe impractical, yet they became a popular heirloom handed down between family members.  
}}

|mat3 = Wool Thread
|mat3qty = 1
|output1 = Bronze Sword
}}

===Recipe tree===
{{Crafting Tree|Bronze Sword}}

==Journal==
{{Journal entry|
Legend has it that when the first sword was forged, Guthix wept. Given that Guthix commissioned warriors and soldiers and, at times, encouraged war, this is perhaps not a truthful statement. Yet, the sword is a weapon with a singular purpose: to end the life of another. Of course, this is true of all weapons, but none so iconic as the blade. 

Children would play in the street, striking one another with wooden blades rather than clubs or daggers. They would pretend to be knights rather than brigands or priests. To wield a blade was a sign of bravery. To draw a sword was the hero’s stance. 

War shatters all childhood dreams. 

Nothing removes the nonsense of chivalry more than seeing the life leaving the eyes of a terrified foe. A sword puts the wielder in that moment, forcing them to face what it is they destroy. To understand that there are no such things as heroes, only warrior against warrior and knowing that one of them will fall. 
}}

==Journal==
{{Journal entry|
Doric would be the first person to praise the simple hammer. It is a large solid chunk of metal attached to a long haft, allowing the weapon to benefit from the unmistakable might of physics. Weight and speed are a terrifying combination. 

Dwarves, in particular, favour this weapon. They feel it speaks to their long-held desire to shape metal by pounding into shape by sheer strength alone. They apply the same logic to their enemies, beating them into a shape that the dwarves feel suits their enemies better.  

There were once dwarves on Ashenfall who were masters of this technique. However, when the humans began their infighting, and Guthix turned away from them, they vanished deeper into the continent, leaving behind a shroud of mystery in search of something extraordinary. 

They found it and have never returned. 
}}

Mouldfinger never wanted to be chief, unlike the other goblins she aspired to be just a background character – happy and forgotten while she worked on her own things. She enjoyed working with wood, bone and, on those special occasions, metal.

She crafted a sword, a simple blade that was sturdy and strong, and the other goblins went wild because of it. Seeing its sharpness, they became convinced it was magical, or imbued with the power of their draconic overlord. It wasn’t, it was just very well made.

She was made chieftain after the others challenged her, and her sharpened blade cut easily through their defenses. A fate she had never wanted, and a fate she could never escape. 
}}

==Journal==
{{Journal entry|
Crystal bows are not crafted but sung into existence. Harmonies are layered over one another to teach the weapon a story so that it might understand why it needs to be strong. Traditionally, the plight of the elves, their loneliness without their goddess, and the many dangers they must face are sung. 

The shimmering substance they’re made from is traditionally a shard of the elven goddess Seren, left behind when her crystal form shattered at the end of the Third Age God Wars. 

This bow isn’t the same material, and the song is different, too. There’s no divine essence in the crystal, no lament of the elves. Instead, it is sung memories of a lifetime together, distilled down into facets reflecting in the crystalline flaws. In the moonlight, those facets sparkle like tiny stars held within glass—moments of hardship, conflict, happiness, and grief. 

It is music as a weapon, with memory as the handle and sorrow as the string. 
}}

Zanik once used this crossbow to defeat a deadly spectre in the underworld. A being infused with some dread malign power, that threatened the denizens of Um. Normal weapons had no effect on the creature, yet for some reason the bone bolts fired by this crossbow were enough to unravel its material form, banishing it back to the darkness.

===Recipe tree===

==Journal==
{{Journal entry|
Rasthin was a powerful sorceress, even among the dragonkin. She had mastered all manner of spells, and was even schooled in the techniques from the previous Revision.

Still, magic as an art was largely looked down upon by dragonkin society. It was considered vulgar, and the obsession of children, and alchemy and science was the real way forward. In part it’s because of the connection between magic and the hated Elder Gods, and the dragonkin feared rousing, further, their anger.

But Rasthin knew the value of magic, as did the other members of her group. She created a staff, forged from glittering crystal and anima-infused iron, which allowed her to channel her magic into powerful spells.

Yet, even with a weapon as powerful as this, she discarded her magic when she uncovered the power of Ashenfall – preferring, instead, to enjoy her newfound connection with elemental flame.

Still, the remnants of the staff bristle with potential, and while they may never reach the might of their ancestor, they remain powerful magic tools just waiting to be crafted.
}}

<!-- Generated by generate_item_pages.py -->

pages.text:

|description = A ferocious blade, filled with primal draconic rage.

}}

==Stats==

|mat4 = Vault Shard

}}

===Recipe tree===
{{Crafting Tree|Dragonbone Dagger}}

==Journal==
{{Journal entry|
Viika was never fond of creating dragons, they thought the process was cruel and unbecoming. When an egg was laid, Viika saw in that moment both the egg and the dragon it would become. They saw past and presence at the same time, and they grieved the creature before it had even hatched.

They pleaded with their sibling, and with the other Dragonkin, to change their treatment of the beasts. But Viika’s curse, like so many Seers, was to be ignored by their kindred.

A rare egg hatched once, containing a dragon with white scales like pearls. The dragon was infused with magic and possessing a power capable of travelling to other worlds, where she might have lived a happier life. But Viika wept, for they knew she would manage no such ending.

Viika kept the hatchling company. Treated them like their dearest friend, and held them softly when Lacrussa’s need for knowledge, took the dragon’s last breath.

The Seer kept only a single keepsake – a blade carved from the tooth of their white-scaled friend. A dagger capable of terrible damage, but as fragile as the friendship that it reminded them of.
}}

|mat3 = Leather
|mat3qty = 2
|output1 = Goblin "Swingslash"
}}

===Recipe tree===
{{Crafting Tree|Goblin "Swingslash"}}

==Journal==
{{Journal entry|
Contrary to popular belief, a goblin is as smart as any other species. It was Bandos that instilled within them the notion that intelligence was a sin, and demonstrating your education was something to be discouraged. As a result, goblins often go out of their way to make themselves seem less intelligent than they really are.

Hence “swingslash”, the name given to this axe because of how it swings through the air, slashing anything it hits. It’s so reductive and silly, it can only be an overcorrection, a desperate attempt by the creator to seem dumber than they are.

You can ignore, therefore the curve of the blade and the sharpness of the edge, created by carefully thought-out techniques that make the weapon deadlier than those before them. The name, chosen by the blacksmith, so none might accuse them of the crime of being smart.
}}

===Recipe tree===
{{Crafting Tree|Granite Maul}}

==Journal==
{{Journal entry|
While metal may be the preferred choice of weapon makers, the granite maul stands as a testament to the raw power of stone. This legendary weapon, revered among hammer wielders, is nothing more than a massive, heavy stone affixed to a stick. Yet, in the hands of a skilled wielder, it becomes a force of unparalleled destruction. 

It was developed by ancient dwarves long ago after one dwarf got fed up with the rock hammer he was using to fight a nest of gargoyles. The beasts were strong and numerous, and their skin was almost impenetrable to conventional weaponry; only rock hammers seemed to hurt them. In frustration, one dwarf took the biggest hunk of granite he could find and turned it into a legend. 

Since that moment, the weapon has been refined, using various special components to make it more wieldy in combat. The original creator having done himself an injury with the recoil. 
}}

pages.text:
{{Infobox Item
|name = Grieving Moon Staff
|image = Grieving Moon Staff.png
|type = Magic Weapon
|description = This staff is dedicated to Lougrim, the fallen Garou goddess of the Moon.
|weight = 15.0
}}
'''Grieving Moon Staff''' is a weapon item.

==Stats==
{{Infobox Weapon
| power = 3
| durability = 1050
| attackstyle = Staff
| damagemulti = 2.6
| criticalchance = 0.15
}}
[[File:Grieving Moon Staff (Equipped).jpg|thumb|An Grieving Moon Staff as seen when equipped by a player]]

==Recipe==
{{Recipe
|facility = Smithing Anvil
|skill = Artisan
|skillxp = 125
|mat1 = Oak Logs
|mat1qty = 8
|mat2 = Antler
|mat2qty = 6
|mat3 = Animal Bone
|mat3qty = 4
|output1 = Grieving Moon Staff
}}

===Recipe tree===
{{Crafting Tree|Grieving Moon Staff}}

==Journal==
{{Journal entry|
Lougrim was a moon deity, born to a cold world of soft silvers and silence. Her people lived lives of quiet contemplation, each moment spent wondering about the next. They were passive, soft spoken and sedentary, which drove Lougrim wild.

When a doorway to another world appeared and a grieving god wandered through it, Lougrim took the opportunity to run. To flee to another place, and live a life that she could endure.

History skips the next bit. Her closest allies have never learned what elevated her from her previous form to that of the goddess of the hunt, but whatever it is was profound enough to change her completely. She no longer resembled her silent kindred. She was quiet, yes, but in the way a tiger is moments before the kill. She is contemplative, true, but it was the stillness of a spider waiting for its prey.

When she found the garou, she knew she had found her real kindred. They were violent and deadly, and they yearned for the hunt. When she took her place as their goddess, she focused and refined them, creating hunters that were capable of felling almost any prey.

Yet still she kept the moon as her symbol, a reminder of her home and how far she has come since leaving it.

Now her priests bear the symbol, atop their staves and as the focus on their altars. They accept and revere Kuldra as their High Queen, yet they grieve the moon goddess that made them who they are today.
}}

==Journal==
{{Journal entry|
Gahl was a garou druid who lost everyone he knew. In the wars inflicted on his people by the other races of Ashenfall, he grew jaded with the cruelty he saw all around him. This reached its peak when he found a garou village in ruins and goblins dancing and laughing as they threatened a newly born pup, in her crib.

The village is gone now, dragged beneath the soil. Gahl’s vengeance unleashed upon the goblins, erasing them from existence. He took the child as his own and raised her in a way alien to his people – he raised her with love.

She had no talent for magic, and could not learn his druid spells, so he taught her how to hunt with a bow, and she was a natural. Dahnu, as he named her, made a bow out of the first deer she and her father had hunted and that bow became a symbol to all she encountered.

In time she would claim a king’s seat. Defeating the prior monarch with reason and wit. Charming him out of his power and offering her people another way.  

Her people thrived, making her father proud.

Until Velgar brought her reign to an end.
}}

|mat4 = Vault Core
|mat4qty = 1
|output1 = Imaru's Talon
}}

===Recipe tree===
{{Crafting Tree|Imaru's Talon}}

==Journal==
{{Journal entry|
The essence of an undead dragon, distilled down into a blade. What could go wrong?

This weapon is cold to the touch in ways far beyond chilling. Holding it feels as if you’re out in the cold, with no shelter to protect you. Exposure in the palm of your hand.

Worse than the cold, is the hunger. This scimitar wants to cut, to stab, to slice and to maim. It wants to see your enemies bleed so that it can feast on their suffering. Imaru may be dead, but her hunger is eternal and you feel it infecting you with each swing of the blade.

Still, the power of this blade makes it almost worth it. What’s a little hunger when faced with the destruction of your foes? Lament later, surely, and revel now in the carnage. Perhaps there’s a little dragon in you too?
}}

pages.text:
{{Infobox Item
|name = Iron Crossbow
|image = Iron Crossbow.png
|type = Ranged Weapon
|description = A 1-Handed Crossbow made from Iron.
|weight = 10.0
}}
'''Iron Crossbow''' is a weapon item.

==Stats==
{{Infobox Weapon
| power = 4
| durability = 1300
| damagetype = Ranged
| attackstyle = Crossbow
| damagemulti = 2
}}
[[File:Iron Crossbow (Equipped).jpg|thumb|An Iron Crossbow as seen when equipped by a player]]

|facility = Fletching Bench
|skill = Artisan
|skillxp = 150

|mat5 = Vault Shard
|mat5qty = 4

}}

===Recipe tree===
{{Crafting Tree|Iron Crossbow}}

==Journal==
{{Journal entry|
Iron turned the war against the garou more than some people would like to admit. They would claim superior training, tactics, and skills of their soldiers, but the truth is far simpler. Iron was strong and iron weapons were brutal.

Ranged weapons proved effective against the garou leadership, bound as they were by strict codes of honour. A thane was a deadly opponent up close, but if you could pierce their internal organs before they were within a swing’s reach, then they were no more dangerous than any other beast. It is perhaps no surprise then, that there was a race to create weapons to challenge them.

The people of Brynmoor had learned of a Wild Knight invention, a weapon that would allow one to deliver powerful ranged attacks, whilst still allowing the bearer to wield a shield. They took this design, studied and refined it and then upgraded it to include the power of iron weapons.

Garou numbers dropped rapidly as a result, turning the war heavily against them. In their desperation, the garou turned to their goddess and she, in turn, sought allies with deadlier weapons still.
}}

Iron daggers are dangerous weapons in the hands of someone skilled. Light, quick, and sharp as can be, they cleave flesh from bone before the victim knows they’ve been assaulted. This is why they became a favourite weapon of the Children of Shadows. 

Though the cult rarely harmed anyone -- it was mostly a social club -- there were those among their number who took their calling seriously. 

They smith daggers themselves, and as the blade cooled, they added a single drop of their own blood in honour of their master. Some carved demonic pacts into the blades, though none are known to actually create such a contract.
}}

pages.text:

|weight = 25.0

==Journal==
{{Journal entry|
Few sights are as intimidating as a great axe hefted toward you. It can only be carried in a pose that exudes raw strength, and a bearer of such a weapon can rarely look meek. 

Bandos was also a fan of the aesthetic. While he favoured the sheer brutality of the kyzaj, he chose to wield a great axe against Velgar. He stormed Brakka’s Folly, great axe in hand, and challenged the dragon to single combat. A god versus a dragon, the outcome felt obvious, yet it did not play out how Bandos expected. 

It should have been easy. Yet Velgar’s stone-touched flesh turned away blows, and his poisonous breath leeched even Bandos’ strength. Bandos cut a great scar down Velgar’s side, yet the wound was not fatal, and the counterattack sent Bandos reeling. 

He never surrendered, but there was no victory for the big high war god. He sounded a tactical retreat, seeking superior tactics – far, far away. 
}}

{{Journal entry|
The iron greatsword is a devastating weapon favoured by the most powerful warriors. The strongest garou warriors wielded them in battle against goblin invaders, turning armies of green-skinned soldiers into nothing but viscera.

When the dragons turned on their allies, these blades would be turned against armoured scales. Several dragons underestimated the power of these weapons in the hands of a skilled wielder. Garou thanes tore young dragons to pieces, wearing their bones as symbols of their success.

It made them an excellent target for the older dragons. Velgar, a mighty general, marked those wearing dragon bone adornments for death. He crushed them first, brutally and swiftly. Their strength shattered beneath his claws or swallowed in an instant.

The practice quickly fell out of favour. 
}}

==Journal==

While it might be cheaper to use bronze, there are some times when sheer power is necessary. For those times, a lumberjack need look no further than the glittering might of an iron axe’s blade. The mightiest of Ghornfell trees falls before its onslaught, and none in that region may stand against its might.

Iron was so powerful, that it created a problem in Brynmoor. The population craved the metal so desperately that they mined the region almost dry. This let to infighting, small civil skirmishes and the white knights of the Blue Flame Keep, needed to be brought in to keep the peace.

It is said that where iron goes, blood soon follows.
}}

{{Crafting Tree|Iron Mace}}

==Journal==
{{Journal entry|
Few instruments are as good at shattering bones as the iron mace. It was a favourite weapon of White Knight paladins, who dreamed of shattering the bones of the risen dead. Necromancy was rare on Ashenfall, though not unheard of. Two necromancers had been reported to the order, one a girl with a knack for bones and another who whispered with the dead. Neither summoned skeletal hordes for the knights to shatter. 

But garou and goblin bones broke just as well. In the war against the garou, Sir Lear, a white knight paladin, used the mace to great effect. He crushed warriors, battered druids, and shattered the shields of powerful thanes. He was a symbol of the might of Saradomin’s faithful when faced with overwhelming odds. 

He joined the Wild Knight commander, Vemmeck, in their siege against a northern garou stronghold. With countless goblins at their side, their victory was almost assured. Yet a single druid’s magic held the forces at bay until Sir Lear learned how ineffective his mace was against the scales of a dragon. 
}}

An iron pickaxe is one such tool. The blade of the axe is hard and precise, delivering massive force at the point of impact. Few ores and stones can resist this power, which is why dwarves have long since favoured the iron pickaxe.

===Recipe tree===

==Journal==
{{Journal entry|
It was iron that nearly saw the end of the garou. Swords forged of the metal were death in handheld form. 

The blacksmiths of Brynmoor churned out countless legions of iron swords, eradicating the village's iron supplies. Each soldier was equipped with such a weapon, and each warrior was ready to cut through the horde. 

The garou are strong, dangerous, and deadly. Their weapons are powerful, and their strikes fierce. Even the magic of their druids puts the wizard's guild to shame, yet their armour fails to turn away iron's wrath. As humans advanced, iron weapons in hand, they razed villages to the ground and wiped out whole families. 

Lougrim wept for the loss of her children, and her cries changed the course of the war. 

Iron is strong, but it melts at great temperatures, and there are no flames as strong as a dragon's breath. As Lougrim bound herself to the service of the Cabal, all that would be left of the villagers would be puddles of molten metal. 
}}

==Journal==
{{Journal entry|
Imcando dwarves preferred the forge to the battlefield but were not afraid to join the frontlines if their aid was needed. They waded into conflict with warhammers held high, ready to smash their enemies out of existence. 

Only one Imcando dwarf made her way to Ashenfall, and she came here for vengeance. Her foes were not the garou or the dragon army, but the green-skinned goblins who had earned her ire. 

She was given a wide berth in Bramblemead, partially because of the enormous hammer hefted across her shoulder but mostly because of the fury that radiated out of her as if her hate were flames and she was perpetually burning. In the rare times the dwarf spoke, she simply mentioned that they were looking for someone the goblins had taken, and she would never rest until she got them back. 
}}

==Journal==
{{Journal entry|
The Children of Shadows were never a grateful sort, but they would acknowledge that the groves of maple in Dowdun Reach were a boon. Almost everything wooden in the keep - from the library bookcases to the bannisters - was made from maple. 

Alric relished walking among the maple trees. For so much of his life, he had been encased in stone and surrounded by essence: working long hours in the keep. But the maples were for thinking. 

In his twilight years, Alric carried a maple staff with him: the Subjugation Staff. It was a crutch for an old man, yes, but it was also a way to bring the grove with him. When he couldn't muster the energy to visit his favoured trees, he carried a remnant with him.  
}}

Bramblemead was often the venue for archery competitions, and the maple longbows would be out in force. Etched into the trophy, you would find many Rasmodels. They were a noble bloodline on Ashenfall, and would often add to their reputation by earning combat trophies.
}}

==Journal==
{{Journal entry|
Putrilius, the cthonian demon, will tell you that any living thing can be possessed. If needs must, a cthonian can be a cockroach or spider, for a time. 

In one desperate moment, after the Dreaming Stone was shattered, Putrilius was forced to possess a maple tree. It wasn't an experience she enjoyed. Most curiously, she found themselves aligning to the maple's inner cadence. Everything slowed. Putrilius barely registered a thought in two years.

What woke Putrilius was a logging axe. Their maple body was toppled and then fletched into an armature. For a moment, as Putrilius's life faded from the wood, she was a shortbow. 

It was the closest she had come to dying. With a cognitive spark, she moved from the bow to a passing hound before all her lifeforce ebbed away.
}}

A mithril crossbow is the standard issue for any modern Black Knight. If you showed capability, then you might be promoted to Black Knight Sniper, and that came with the Black Sniper Crossbow. But for the average Black Knight, the crossbow was a long-kept friend. 

The crossbow's main purpose was to stop demons in their tracks. The Zamorakian magi claimed to keep their demon hordes under control, but demons tested that claim.

The crossbow had other uses. Remove the bolt, rest the crossbow on the floor stock-upwards, and you had a stool. Sit on the floor and it was a headrest. 

You could also waste time shooting ravens. Shooting the red, glowing ravens was an immediate dismissal, however. Alric demanded they were protected, for reasons that he never expanded on.
}}

{{Crafting Tree|Mithril Dagger}}

{{Journal entry|
Doric likes to remind Vannaka that you wouldn't find a dwarf making daggers.

As Doric says, "it's a very human thing, a dagger. A dwarf never hides their weapon: we're polite enough to show what you're up against. An' you won't find us attacking from the back or sides. No sir, we bonk you from the front."
}}

In the early years of Dowdun Reach, the concept of a mithril greataxe would have been unthinkable. A big, showy weapon that used large quantities of mithril would have been seen as a betrayal. The Children of Shadows, holding a monopoly on mithril, wanted to present it as a rare metal. At best, the richest on Ashenfall might have a brooch or tiara that sparkled with mithril. 

Nowadays, Doric can have two greataxes whipped up for you before dinner. Much has changed. 
}}

This time, however, the joke was on Vannaka. Doric had made his mam's rock cakes, and folded some mithril round them - one for each hilt. 

Vannaka wobbled. "Cripes," he said. 
}}

That is likely to change, of course, as more adventurers make landfall. It makes you wonder what the island will look like in five, ten years' time as the cultures intertwine.

===Recipe tree===

==Journal==
{{Journal entry|
The mithril warhammer was once the weapon most associated with Lord Rasmodel of Dowdun Reach. He was rarely seen without it. In the mornings, when he was most in pain, it would be a prop, something to lean on while the night's watch recounted the events of the early hours. In the afternoons, it was a beautiful warning, a threat of what might happen if you disobeyed him.

Lord Rasmodel wasn't one for naming his weapons. He found the custom a little juvenile. But when his thoughts were elsewhere, you might see him pat the head of the warhammer. There was kinship there, even if he wouldn't admit it. 
}}

pages.text:

|name = Necromancer's Staff
|image = Necromancer's Staff.png
|type = Magic Weapon
|description = Necromantic essence still eminates from this staff.
|weight = 15.0
}}
'''Necromancer's Staff''' is a weapon item.

==Stats==
{{Infobox Weapon

| durability = 1050
| attackstyle = Staff
| damagemulti = 5.9
}}

==Recipe==
{{Recipe

|mat4 = Vault Shard
|mat4qty = 6
|output1 = Necromancer's Staff
}}

===Recipe tree===
{{Crafting Tree|Necromancer's Staff}}

==Journal==
{{Journal entry|
Necromancy isn’t inherently evil, despite what some of the scriptures might say. Whilst some cruelly force the dead into service, others make bargains and pacts with spirits, offering them a chance to influence the mortal world again, rather than simply watch events unfold.

In Fellhollow a small group of mages encountered a smoking fissure in the earth. From it they heard strange whispering voices in the darkness, promising them power and knowledge. Greedily they listened, making pacts with the whispering in exchange for the ability to bind the dead to their service.

In life they performed their arts in secret, raising corpses none would miss, or binding ghosts no one even knew were haunting them. Yet in death, that’s where their true power lay.

With their souls withered away and consumed by the dread Imaru, the necromancers were no longer limited by the last vestiges of morality, and with the new powers at their command, turned a struggling defence against the undead, into a horror-filled nightmare where each fallen ally would rise as a foe.
}}

<!-- Generated by generate_item_pages.py -->

pages.title: User:RSDWArchive/Weapon/Oak Battlestaff
pages.text:
{{Infobox Item
|name = Oak Battlestaff
|image = Oak Battlestaff.png
|type = Magic Weapon
|description = A magical weapon carved from oak wood.
|weight = 15.0
}}

==Stats==
{{Infobox Weapon

| durability = 1050
| attackstyle = Staff
| damagemulti = 2.8
}}
[[File:Oak Battlestaff (Equipped).jpg|thumb|An Oak Battlestaff as seen when equipped by a player]]

==Recipe==

|skill = Artisan
|skillxp = 125

|mat2qty = 6
|mat3 = Wool Thread
|mat3qty = 3
|output1 = Oak Battlestaff
}}

===Recipe tree===
{{Crafting Tree|Oak Battlestaff}}

==Journal==
{{Journal entry|
Saradominist mages preferred the hefty swing of an oak staff over the more pliable ash. They felt that sturdier oak allowed for more powerful spells. The more durable wood was less prone to shattering itself into splinters when they cast their most devastating magic. 

While most wizards practised familiar spells, pushing their research into different practical applications for what was already known, one wizard would push the boundaries of spellcasting. Her name was Cynthia Valtis, and she would go down in local legend.

She would have liked to be remembered for her ability to transform twigs into carrots, thus preventing the famine that threatened Brynmoor. She would have wanted to be remembered for her clever application of wind and water magic to create a nice cool breeze during the summer heat. But, alas, she would be remembered for an ill-advised fire magic experiment that would see her travel across the continent faster than any wizard before. 

Her body has yet to be found. 
}}

|type = Ranged Weapon
|description = A powerful longbow carved from oak wood.
|weight = 15.0
}}

}}

===Recipe tree===
{{Crafting Tree|Oak Longbow}}

==Journal==
{{Journal entry|
The longbow is every hunter’s friend. Capable of taking down prey over a greater range than the shortbow, it has been the end of many a deer trying to flee through the woods. 

Bramblemead would host a thrilling archery tournament. The villagers would proudly bring their own longbows, meticulously crafted by hand, and compete to take down cleverly hidden targets scattered across the village. It was a spectacle of skill and precision, with the only rule being that the target must be shootable from the open street. 

With the introduction of magic, the competition took on a whole new level of silliness. Participants conjured magical air currents that sent arrows astray – often into unsuspecting washing lines or pies cooling on windowsills – or they would playfully blast arrows out of the air with fire spells. 

However, this stopped after the Amalgamated archer, Derien, joined the game. Gifted with the element of air, his arrows curved gracefully with each shot, missing traps, tricks and deceptions. His victory earned the Amalgamated more jealousy, which led to the competition ending out of envious spite.
}}

<!-- Generated by generate_item_pages.py -->

===Recipe tree===

==Journal==
{{Journal entry|
The villagers who proved their skills with an ash shortbow were brought into the forest by a Wild Knight instructor in search of more durable wood. The oak stood proud and tall in the forest, standing firm against the ebb of time. Villagers would gather the sturdiest branches that they could and be brought to a quiet place in the wood to begin carving their new bows. 

The Wild Knight would tell them that a good bow is more than just a weapon, but an extension of oneself. They encouraged each villager to put a piece of themselves into the bow, a decoration on the handle, or a poem carved into the wood. Whatever made the bow personal to them. 

This connection in the crafting process would help the villagers feel comfortable with their new weapon, and many young hunters would use the same bow throughout their lives. It even became a tradition for young hunters to gift a bow to their beloved as a marriage proposal. A tradition that survived into the third age.
}}

==Journal==
{{Journal entry|
Climb the turrets of Dowdun Reach in its prime, and you would have found the Black Knight Snipers. These were the elite marksmen of the Black Knights, tasked with looking outward when the rest of the order would look inward. They would scan the skyline for threat - for the beating wings of a dragon. There was no greater trophy for the Black Snipers.

One one occasion, a bolt was loosed at Imaru. She had arrived at the keep for diplomatic purposes, and was rewarded with a bolt to the neck. You could see the conflict within her, as her urges were swallowed. For a second, a disdainful eye moved to the Black Sniper. But she reset and continued to talk to Lord Rasmodel and Alric like nothing happened.

Restraint was certainly beaten into the Black Snipers that night. 
}}

==Journal==
{{Journal entry|
Garou children would often play pretend at great, epic, battles. They would create their own stories, true, but they enjoyed reenacting the battles of legend, and no character was more popular than King Raluh, the mighty.

Pups would wander into the forests in search of the sturdiest stick they could find and then announce to the other children that they were the king, and this stick that most legendary of weapons – Skullsplitter.

As the legends go, King Raluh had been granted a weapon by the moon goddess, Lougrim. A weapon capable of destroying any foe. Armour was as paper to Skullsplitter and the fables had him crush the bones of villain and monster alike.

It is, therefore, no surprise that as the garou pups mature, they create weapons to match the legends, and garou warriors wield mighty maces carrying that mythical name. Though the true Skullsplitter may be just a story, these powerful imitations are no less deadly.
}}

pages.text:
{{Infobox Item
|name = Splitbark Staff
|image = Splitbark Staff.png
|type = Magic Weapon
|description = A Mage's Weapon crafted from Wither rotted Hollow Bark. It has Strange Magical Properties.
|weight = 15.0
}}
'''Splitbark Staff''' is a weapon item.

==Stats==
{{Infobox Weapon
| power = 5
| durability = 1300
| attackstyle = Staff
| damagemulti = 5.4
}}
[[File:Splitbark Staff (Equipped).jpg|thumb|An Splitbark Staff as seen when equipped by a player]]

==Recipe==
{{Recipe
|facility = Smithing Anvil
|skill = Artisan
|skillxp = 250

|mat1qty = 16
|mat2 = Sacred Oil
|mat2qty = 6

|mat3qty = 4
|mat4 = Vault Shard
|mat4qty = 6
|output1 = Splitbark Staff
}}

===Recipe tree===
{{Crafting Tree|Splitbark Staff}}

==Journal==
{{Journal entry|
Magic always inspires people. Something about rewriting the underpinning rules of your reality, with little more than force of will and a handful of stones, makes people reach for lofty goals. In Fellhollow it was the wizard Falistrius that wanted to make a name for themselves.

They wanted to be a magical scholar. A researching who might uncover the secrets of the universe itself. They sought spells of greater and greater power, dabbling in mysteries both profane and forbidden, and came up empty handed.

After constantly searching for power, it was an unexpected duel that would grant her the fame she sought, at a price she would have preferred to avoid. A travelling elven sorcerer arrived in the village of Emberwood one night and the local drunk foolishly picked a fight with him. Unfortunately for Falistrius that drunk was her wife.

The elf challenged the drunk to a duel, for satisfaction over a drink spilled, and by local custom Falistrius was forced to answer. They met in the forests of Fellhollow, and unleashed their magic.

The elf’s spells were like nothing she had ever seen before. Cold and dark, sanguine and terrible, they tore into her magical defences like they were nothing and writhed inside her soul like a foul nightmare. Against the strain, her staff shattered, leaving her vulnerable and in desperation she grasped a fallen hollow tree branch and urged her magic through it.

It wasn’t enough to save her. But it was enough to earn the respect and satisfaction of the sorcerer, who left the region with his pale-skinned cohorts. Ever since then, young Fellhollow mages would craft staves from the hollow bark from the forest, in memory of Falistrius.
}}

==Stats==

===Recipe tree===
{{Crafting Tree|Staff of Light}}

==Journal==
{{Journal entry|
In the third age, before someone could wield a staff of light, it would first need to be blessed by three people. 

A priest would bless it first, instilling the weapon with Saradomin’s wisdom. A monk would bless it second, imbuing the staff with the humility of Saradomin. Finally, an icyene general would give their blessing, dedicating the weapon to Saradomin’s righteousness. 

Those battlemages chosen to wield such a sacred weapon would dive into battle against Zamorak’s forces and believe themselves invulnerable. 

They were often proved wrong. 

Yet, those who carried the staff into battle seemed to survive longer than their compatriots. The defensive magic of the staff kept them alive longer than others. A demon once stole a staff and tried to use that power for himself. Still, when he tried to channel demonic power through the staff, it is said that the explosion could be seen from across the battlefield.
}}

==Journal==
{{Journal entry|
There were no garou in Fellhollow for the people to deal with, so in the early days of the settlement people created steel crossbows primarily for hunting game. The dragons that guarded the people were gentle creatures, but possessed of an enormity so great that they required a great deal of food to keep going. Hunting, therefore, became a very popular activity, with many competitions and events crafted to provide meals for the draconic caretakers. 

When the withering began to spread and the dead began to rise, steel bolts proved just as effective at piercing a zombie’s skull, as it would catching the quarry from a hunt. 

Though the dead would come in droves, the crossbow would let them be taken out at great distance, keeping the last remaining settlements safter for just a few days longer. Every moment counted in those final days. 
}}

The children of darkness were very fond of steel daggers. They were powerful, dangerous and very expensive at the time, making them the perfect ritual tool to demonstrate their adoration of the god of chaos.

Mostly the offspring of wealthy families, the rituals of the Children were performed out of boredom more than religious fervour. They would gather at secret meetings and proudly display their elaborately carved daggers, trying to prove to each other who was the most devoted to the lord of darkness by way of expensive kitchenware.

If you asked any of them to use such a blade in battle, they would be more inclined to slit their own throats in fear, rather than mutilate their opponents.
}}

==Journal==
{{Journal entry|
Power corrupts, and what’s more powerful than an enormous axe comprised of a metal stronger than any seen before? Such was the logic of Olga Skulltaker (not her real name), the self-styled bandit queen of Fellhollow.

As a child, Olga had always enjoyed listening to her grandfather’s stories from the mainland. Tales of mighty warriors defeating terrifying foes. Purehearted heroes challenging evil knights to great battles across pointlessly high bridges and the like. Her parents always laughed and called her “their little hero” but the truth was she was far more interested in the villain.

When she was old enough to wield a weapon, she trained with the biggest axes she could carry, and her sparring partners feared facing off against her because she always took things too far. Once even costing another student their fingers.

As an adult, she found a place for herself among the Children of Shadows. She had hoped they would be dangerous, wicked people, like herself, but instead they spend their time reciting terrible poetry and talking about evil, without ever doing it.

So she took her axe, changed her name, and found the highest bridge she could, and attacked anyone that tried to cross it.
}}

Sadly for the villagers, the art of godsword creation was never discovered, so they turned to steel instead. Whilst significantly less flashy, and almost certainly not going to terrify any deities, a steel sword will do significant damage to anything not divinely attuned.
}}

Although the Saradominist priests had first insisted that different faiths could only turn on one another, they found an odd sort of kinship in their fellows and would regularly enjoy debates of the merits and flaws of each other’s faiths.

==Journal==
{{Journal entry|
Metal is difficult to mine with inferior tools. A lesson that one dwarf tried very hard not to learn. Baralas, a dwarf from Keldagrim, once refused to trade up his bronze pickaxe for anything else. He was convinced, that any metal could mine any other metal, because what he was targeting was the stone, not the ore itself. An interesting plan, and one with tragic results. 

While mining a rare metal with a particularly solid structure, his pick struck a single piece of the ore, no bigger than a pebble, and his bronze pick shattered into countless pieces as a result. Like an explosion made from tiny little metallic shards, Baralas was shredded into nothing but the lingering stench of regret. 

Now, dwarves make it their business to find the hardest, strongest and most impressive pickaxes out there, lest they become as much a stain on history as Baralas was on the mine's wall. 
}}

It is said that the origins of the scimitar come from Al Kharid, where skilled swordsmen would wield them in a dazzling manner akin to a dance. Travelling dignitaries would be wowed by the spectacle, applauding great choreographed sword clashes that dramatized legendary battles of history’s past.

They were brought to Ashenfall by a noble family during the god wars, who had taken up the scimitar as their weapon of choice, in service, of course, of the mighty Saradomin. This family was proud and pious, which made them all the more concerned when their daughter was one of the first to show signs of Amalgamation.

Love, for once, triumphed over prejudice, and the family scorned their wealth and prestige in order to protect their child and they joined many others who fled the growing unrest in the village of Bramblemead and travelled north to Fellhollow. There, they taught the secrets of the scimitar to the local blacksmiths, and the weapon found itself a great deal of popularity in the times to come.
}}

==Journal==
{{Journal entry|
Atilius Rasmodel was a proud and powerful knight. Her skill with the sword was without compare and her bravery seemingly infinite against the dangers in Fellhollow. She was chosen by Imaru as one of her guards, not that a dragon needed such a thing, and she was made responsible for the defence of the dragons when they fell into the weeks long torpors they were known to suffer.

She called herself the Dragon Knight, even though such a title was unofficial and self-proclaimed, it soon stuck among the village people and they would bow and nod in appreciation of her skill as they passed her.

When Kuldra descended on the region, demanding humanity’s destruction, Atilius climbed on Xikotal’s back in hope to drive her steel blade through the Godeater’s heart. She died beside the dragon she had sworn to protect, free of all remorse, in the line of duty.
}}

==Journal==
{{Journal entry|
Lionel wasn’t good at much in life, but he could swing a hammer and he could swing it hard. As one of the Amalgamated, born with hair of metal and skin of stone, Lionel worried that he would be as hated as the Amalgamated in Brynmoor had been, but Fellhollow proved a kinder place.

Instead of being feared and shunned, his unnatural strength became a source of much pride in his family and friends. Locals would challenge him to heft heavier and heavier things, cheering and lavishing him with praise as he did so.

Life had been good. He was happy. So, of course, it all had to end.

When the dead began to rise, arrows and blades seemed less effective than before, but crushing force still got the job done. His family and friends escaped to the Silverthorn Keep and Lionel waited at the guardhouse, standing as a wall against the tide of the undead. Countless zombies and skeletons fell to his hammer, reduced to little more than powder in a few careful swings. He could have stemmed the tide if that were all, but when the Zogre descended upon him, even Lionel’s Warhammer eventually broke against the strain.
}}

{{Crafting Tree|Stone Club}}

==Journal==
{{Journal entry|
A big stone attached to a stick; not terribly durable, but surprisingly handy. This simple weapon has saved the lives of many warriors. When caught between a rock and a hard place, why not take advantage of the rock? 

Goblin children play a game called bonk-bonk-bash, a rather unpleasant little pastime that acts as a form of population control. Each of the goblins takes a rock, attaches it to a stick and then takes it in turns to thwack their friends with it. This is a traditional household game that keeps the costs of feeding the family low. 
}}

===Recipe tree===
{{Crafting Tree|Stone Dagger}}

==Journal==
{{Journal entry|
One of the first weapons ever created, the stone dagger has a sinister history. At first it allowed early humans, dwarves, elves and other intelligent species to thrive. They could take down prey and feast on the spoils, using the dagger to pare down the kill, making it easier to eat. It created the gap between animals and people, the first step toward civilization. 

It’s also the first tool to lead to murder. 

A young man whose name has long been forgotten once created a dagger of glittering black stone. Pleased with his work, he proudly displayed it, earning admiration and envy from those around him. 
So proud was he of the dagger that he would whisper to it, acting as though it would whisper back. On the day he slit the chieftain’s throat, he claimed that the dagger had told him to do it. 

Fortunately most stone daggers are pleasingly silent.
}}

<!-- Generated by generate_item_pages.py -->

{{Crafting Tree|Stone Logging Axe}}

==Journal==
{{Journal entry|
Trees are arrogant creatures, standing tall in the forests and refusing to bow to the wind. For decades they spread their roots claiming dominion over the nearby soil. The claws of beasts are a mere distraction. Raging storms barely rustle their leaves. They refuse to move for anyone, arrogant to the core.

Yet, it is not the wind, nor the heavens that shatter their resolve. Indeed, it is the lowliest of stones, refined to an edge. No matter how old the tree, how sturdy its fame, a single stone, the size of your fist, can bring the mightiest ash low.  

The stone axe is an alliance of such a rock, working together with branches cast aside in the tree’s hubris. Proof that even the lowliest of us can make a difference.
}}

Such is the curse of the stone pickaxe, whose role in life is to strike at its brethren, cutting large rocks into pieces, so that they might better be put to use. The smaller stone, carved so sharply that it has nothing left but the edge, turns the action of a single swing into an force that can shatter the largest boulder. There’s a metaphor there, I’m sure.
}}

{{Journal entry|
Anyone who worked with Alric knew about the Subjugation Staff. It would thud, thud, thud on the cobblestone floor as he approached. It was a welcome warning that Alric was arriving, but it came with notes of dread. 

The power of the Subjugation Staff came from what was rolled up and inserted into its middle. It was a demon pact, but one that had been defaulted on. A demon, not a human, had failed to keep their side of the bargain, and that gave the contract a radiating power. Alric captured that power within the staff. While he held it, it was as if all of demonkind owed him something.
}}

==Journal==
{{Journal entry|
Might makes right. This is the truth of the Garou philosophy. Strength and cunning dominates all. The Garou thanes demonstrate this might in the weapons they wield. Though heavier and more tiring, this axe swings with deadly force, capable of cleaving through almost any armour.

The design is passed down to the garou from templates granted to the first garou blacksmiths by divine revelation. Lougrim taught them about the curve of the blade, and the heft of the hilt. Designed for the hunt, it could fell most quarry in a single blow.

Under Kuldra’s leadership, the chosen quarry was human. The dragon queen had a deep hatred for the humans, she found them weak, petty and flawed. The Thanes carried out her orders, using the axe to wield their authority, in her name.
}}

Lord Rasmodel has been put to rest at last.

He rarely knew quiet. His family pushed and cajoled him to further the Rasmodel name. Then he was beset by nightmares, as two malevolent beings grappled over his mind. When the Dreaming Stone shattered, they all took him. He was their spoils. 

We can only hope that, when death came, there was quietus. He felt it for a moment as the Dreaming Stone fell, and - perhaps - he feels it on the journey over the Noumenon. 

The part of him that was a titan - that part remains. You hold it in your hands. Unleash its wrath.
}}

pages.title: User:RSDWArchive/Weapon/Ulv's Longbow
pages.text:
{{Infobox Item
|name = Ulv's Longbow
|image = Ulv's Longbow.png
|type = Ranged Weapon
|description = Once the bow of a silent hunter. Deals additional damage to unaware targets.
|weight = 15.0
}}
'''Ulv's Longbow''' is a weapon item.

==Stats==
{{Infobox Weapon
| power = 5
| durability = 1300
| damagetype = Ranged
| attackstyle = Longbow
| damagemulti = 2.7
}}
[[File:Ulv's Longbow (Equipped).jpg|thumb|An Ulv's Longbow as seen when equipped by a player]]

==Recipe==
{{Recipe
|facility = Fletching Bench
|skill = Artisan
|skillxp = 300
|mat1 = Willow Logs
|mat1qty = 12
|mat2 = Fine Thread
|mat2qty = 8
|mat3 = Animal Bone
|mat3qty = 10
|mat4 = Green Dragonhide Leather
|mat4qty = 4
|mat5 = Dwellberries
|mat5qty = 12
|output1 = Ulv's Longbow
}}

===Recipe tree===
{{Crafting Tree|Ulv's Longbow}}

==Journal==
{{Journal entry|
Ulv was always the silent hunter, in more ways than one. While it was true that no word ever left his lips, his footsteps, breath and, most importantly, his arrows, were all equally devoid of sound. The Wild Knights would jokingly call him the Silent Killer – though over time the crueler members of the order called him the Silent But Deadly, knowing he lacked the capability to argue back. The boy took these insults with quiet grace, never biting back, never reacting. He was used to those little cruelties, having faced them all his life. 

He had carved the bow himself and once walked Ravanna through the process. The two of them were as true a friendship as could ever be hoped for. No lingering romantic tensions. No transaction or debt. No honour chained them together. They were friends simply because they were. Because two lonely people found each other and found a comfort in each others eccentricities. The wolf and the bat, platonic soulmates to the end. 

For one of his birthdays, Ravanna enchanted the bow with a simple spell. She took animal bones, skulls and hides and wove necromantic magic through them, wrapping them around the limbs and grip of the bow, imbuing them with just enough of the underworld to strip them of their sound and a little of their substance. Like ghosts, the arrows would sail silently through the air, dropping targets before they ever knew what hit them. 

It would have been the perfect birthday gift. If only Ulv had lived long enough to receive it. 

The garou can be brutal in their assaults, considering notions like mercy to be the domain of the weak-willed, or the cowardly. Ulv was simply in the wrong place, at the wrong time, and was ambushed by a patrol of young garou, looking for the bloodmark that would grant them adulthood. There were six of them in total, but Ulv reduced them down to four, before the claws entered his throat and silenced him forever. 

When Ravanna found his body, she went mad. An anger unlike any other, a rage more powerful than any storm, waged a war inside her between the light and the dark. The darkness won. Using the souls of slain garou, Ravanna's necromantic curse tore through the Highlands, twisting the clouds into screaming things, filled with a fraction of the rage she felt. As the ghosts of the dead garou crashed about within them, the clouds unleashed lightning and thunder upon the populace below, destroying countless settlements in an instant and countless more over the centuries that followed. 
}}

<!-- Generated by generate_item_pages.py -->

Yet he had no desire for warfare, and no matter the words of encouragement from the captain, he would never join the guard.

For him, archery was a passion, an art, never to be used against another person. A stance quickly forgotten by his shambling corpse.
}}

There was magic once, in this bow, though it has mostly faded now. Runes were ground down and mixed with plant oils, to create a special resin that allowed the Wild Knights to work their magic through the bow, as if they were carrying runes. That magic has faded away, and the recipe for the mixture lost to the ages, but these bows are still formidable in their own right.

Wild Knights told tales of creatures that lived within the wilds of Ashenfall, that could not be felled by normal bows. Deer with hides that glistened with starlight, sheep who’s silver fleeces repelled all arrows. They claimed that these magically infused bows were the only ones capable of harming the creatures, though none ever returned with trophies to share.
}}

===Recipe tree===

==Journal==
{{Journal entry|
Ulv had many skills, but hunting was his strongest. He was inherently quiet, still as the night, and he could hold his position, in the trees or bushes, for hours at a time. Ravanna was never good at this. When the two of them hunted together, Ravanna would sneeze, cough or scream at the sight of an unexpected insect, and the two of them would watch their quarry disappear off into the night.

Although this would occasionally test their friendship, Ulv never really held it against Ravanna. Instead he continued attempting to teach the necromancer the ways of the forest, and the techniques the Wild Knights would use to track down game. It never took. Try as she might, Ravanna was never going to be silent as a shadow.

In apology, she looked for a way to create a weapon that would reduce the impact of her noisy nature. Longbows fired further and faster, meaning that less stealth was needed. She made her way into Fellhollow in search of a material strong enough, and returned with willow branches that she crafted into a longbow as a gift for her friend.

Their hunts improved dramatically afterward. That was, until they became the hunted.
}}

==Journal==
{{Journal entry|
As the dead rose in greater and greater numbers, the villagers needed stronger weapons to defeat them. Their ash and oak longbows failed to unleash sufficient force needed to stem the relentless tide of the undead. They needed a stronger material, that was also flexible enough to deliver arrows of great enough force.

Willow was their solution. It is said that the first willow shortbow was gifted to a villager by a spirit that dwelled within the grove. Legends say that the spirit had an unnatural beauty, and would lure foolish villagers into the waters where they would be drowned. Yet, somehow, one villager earned not the spirits ire, but their admiration.

The spirit gave up their immortal life, to court the villager. They brought gifts of flowers, fish and finally they gifted a shortbow made from the willow trees in the spirits grove. The validity of this tale is hotly debated, with most assuming it is little more than myth, yet willow groves are still considered spiritual places, where one must be careful who they talk to.
}}

}}

===Recipe tree===
{{Crafting Tree|Wolfbane Dagger}}

==Journal==
{{Journal entry|
Vannaka knows this dagger only too well, for it was used against him every time he tried to prove his mettle against the other slayer masters. Although he would never willingly transform to prove his might, the unsettling sight of the dagger broke his morale enough to make him falter, and that was enough of an edge for his defeat.
}}

===Recipe tree===
{{Crafting Tree|Wooden Training Sword}}

==Journal==
{{Journal entry|
In the village of Bramblemead, children grew up knowing battle. The God Wars hadn’t reached them yet, but the priests always knew that the danger was coming. So children were taught combat in the form of a game.

Carpenters were encouraged to make little wooden swords, so that the children could emulate the noble paladins that would wander the village. These soldiers were held up as the peak aspiration, young men and women daydreamed through school, about taking up those glittering swords and running off into adventure.

These weapons clicked and clacked against one another, harmless it seemed to the adults that watched. But wood in Bramblemead was hardened and preserved by druidic magic, and it soon became clear the damage these swords could deal. Injuries were common, bruises and broken bones were the rite of passage of every child.

It was fortunate, perhaps, that these weapons were so painful – because the children of Bramblemead grew up with a better understanding that war was no game.
}}

==Journal==
{{Journal entry|
There are myths and anecdotes about Zamorak appearing to the inhabitants of Dowdun Reach. Few truly believe them. Zamorak is not one for hiding his presence: he's more theatrical than that.

It may be hard to believe, but some have tried to 'summon' Zamorak to Dowdun Reach. Call it the hubris of a demon summoner: a student feels they have mastered the art of summoning demons, so believes the next rung on the ladder is god. Never mind that Zamorak has no ounce of demon within him.

Zamorak can be accused of many things, but he does have a sense of humour. These attempts have been greeted with a gift. He has been known to kick a bloodveld through the portal, or another creature he has to hand. It gives the demon summoner a little spot-test.
}}

==Journal==
{{Journal entry|
Captain Artorius Flamhanger was a brave man, who stood against the rampaging forces of the damned even as his defences crumbled around him. He never wavered, never faltered, and he never left a man behind.

In a better world, there would be songs about him. But Fellhollow scorns legacy, leaving only ruin and despair where hope might take root.

He held the line against the encroaching horde far longer than anywhere else in the region. Days turned into months and months grew into years. As the rest of the region vanished into death, despair and rot, Captain Flamhanger stood strong. As his children died and rose again, the captain returned them to the earth with sombre compassion. When his wife joined them in death, he gave her a final kiss of polished silver that cleaved her head from her shoulders.

In a better world he might have held back the darkness, stood as the single ray of light in a world of shadow. But this is not our world.

There are no sagas of this man. No great texts telling of his heroic deeds. The only sound he made in the time after his death, is the bubbling song of digestive acids within a zogre’s belly.

History is penned by the survivors and the victors, yet the deeds of the dead echo across time. This axe, simple as it may appear, is infused with vengeance against the dead that had taken so much from  a good man.
}}
